title: StructEval: Benchmarking LLMs' Capabilities to Generate Structural Outputs
authors: Jialin Yang, Dongfu Jiang, Lipeng He, Sherman Siu, Yuxuan Zhang, Disen Liao, Zhuofeng Li, Huaye Zeng, Yiming Jia, Haozhe Wang, Benjamin Schneider, Chi Ruan, Wentao Ma, Zhiheng Lyu, Yifei Wang, Yi Lu, Quy Duc Do, Ziyan Jiang, Ping Nie, Wenhu Chen (20 authors; equal contribution: Jialin Yang, Dongfu Jiang; correspondence: j586yang, dongfu.jiang, wenhuchen, ping.nie @uwaterloo.ca)
venue: arXiv preprint (cs.SE/cs.AI/cs.CL); v1 2025-05-26, v2 2026-01-19, v3 2026-04-02 (fetched). No peer-reviewed venue/journal-ref listed on the arXiv abs page. Project page: https://tiger-ai-lab.github.io/StructEval/
claim: LLMs are systematically less capable at generating structured outputs than at generating fluent free text, and this gap is unevenly distributed: it is worse for generation-from-scratch than for format-to-format conversion, worse for visually-rendered formats (HTML/React/SVG/etc.) than for text-only structures (JSON/YAML/etc.), and worse for less-common formats (TOML, Mermaid, TikZ, Vega) than for common ones (JSON, HTML, Markdown). Even the best model tested (GPT-4o) averages only 76.02% and the best open-source model (Qwen3-4B) trails by about 10 points, with some individual subtasks (e.g., open-source TOML generation) scoring near single digits.
novelty: The paper's stated contribution vs. prior work (Section 5.2/1): existing structured-output benchmarks each cover only part of the problem — SoEval is JSON/XML rule-checking with flat schemas that miss deep-hierarchy errors; StrucText-Eval evaluates reasoning *over* structured text rather than generation; FOFO covers domains like law/finance but few formats and needs human verification; StackEval/CodeXGLUE are programming-artifact-only; Struc-Bench is tabular-only with bespoke metrics. StructEval claims to be the first to unify (a) both non-renderable (JSON/YAML/CSV/TOML/XML/Markdown) and renderable (HTML/React/SVG/Mermaid/TikZ/etc.) formats — 18 formats, 44 task types — (b) both generation (NL→structure) and conversion (structure→structure) paradigms, and (c) fully automated scoring combining syntax validity, keyword/dot-path matching, and VLM-based visual question answering, rather than relying on human verification or format-specific bespoke metrics.

protocol.description: All tasks are evaluated zero-shot with a single uniform prompt template and no interactive/multi-turn feedback loop; each example is one forward call producing one structured output that is then automatically scored (Section 4.1: "All tasks are evaluated in a zero-shot setting using consistent prompts and parameters"; "All tasks are evaluated zero-shot using uniform task prompts defined in Table 4").
protocol.observation_content: The model is shown exactly: (1) the task question q, a natural-language description with itemized 'Feature Requirements' (for generation tasks) or a structured-format input to convert (for conversion tasks), wrapped in the fixed instruction template from Table 4: '{StructEval Question} / IMPORTANT: Only output the required output format. You must start the format/code with <|BEGIN_CODE|> and end the format/code with <|END_CODE|>. No other text output (explanation, comments, etc.) are allowed. / Do not use markdown code fences.' No keywords, VQA pairs, or reference answers are shown to the generating model — those (K and Q^v) are held out and used only by the separate automatic scorer. No few-shot demonstrations, no chain-of-thought instruction, and no per-turn feedback are part of the generation call. A separate downstream call (only for StructEval-V) sends the rendered image plus a VQA prompt template (Figure 5) to GPT-4.1-mini, asking it to answer true/false/null for each QA pair based solely on the image — this is a judge call, not feedback returned to the generating model.

representations: Two paradigm/format axes are compared for effect on accuracy, not literal prompt-representation variants (e.g., JSON-vs-XML-encoded-instructions): (1) StructEval-T (text-only: JSON, XML, YAML, Markdown, CSV, TOML) vs. StructEval-V (renderable: HTML, React, SVG, Mermaid, TikZ, Typst, Vega, Vue, Angular, Canvas, Matplotlib, LaTeX) — models score much lower on StructEval-V than StructEval-T overall (Table 6); (2) generation (NL→structure) vs. conversion (structure→structure) — "models perform better on conversion tasks than on generation tasks" in general (Section 4.2 'Generation vs. Conversion'), with a noted exception: in StructEval-T, commercial models actually do better on generation than conversion while open-source models show the reverse pattern. Format identity itself is the largest swing factor: within StructEval-T generation, TOML depresses open-source scores to single digits (8.58% average under temp=1) while JSON/CSV/YAML/HTML/Markdown are "saturated" (>90% for most models) (Section 4.2 'Subtasks Analysis', Table 8). No ablation of prompt phrasing, key ordering, or serialization-style variants is reported in the paper.

models.name: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
models.key_results: Average 61.77 (Table 6): T-gen 60.22, T-conv 71.26, V-gen 54.44, V-conv 61.15. Second-best open-source average overall (Table 6), but on T→TOML specifically it scores 6.76 (Table 8), which is second-lowest among open-source models — below Meta-Llama-3-8B (7.94), Qwen3-4B (7.44), Qwen2.5-7B (13.22), and Phi-4-mini (16.12), and above only Phi-3-mini (0.00).

models.name: Phi-3-mini-128k-instruct
models.key_results: Lowest average of all 12 models: 40.79 (Table 6): T-gen 47.39, T-conv 29.78 (worst of all models on T-conv), V-gen 44.77, V-conv 41.23. T→TOML = 0.00 (Table 8). Section 4.2: fails via malformed <|END_CODE|> closing tags on TOML→YAML and loses hierarchical CSV→JSON structure.

models.name: Qwen3-4B
models.key_results: Best open-source model overall: average 67.04 (Table 6), T-gen 64.95, T-conv 81.13, V-gen 57.00, V-conv 65.08. Trails GPT-4o by ~10 points (Section 4.2 'Overall Performance'). Δ(o1-mini−Qwen3-4B) row in Table 6: T-gen 23.17, T-conv 0.70, V-gen 4.99, V-conv 5.32, Average 8.54.

models.name: Gemini-1.5-pro
models.key_results: Average 71.75 (Table 6): T-gen 88.07, T-conv 74.24, V-gen 58.11, V-conv 66.59. T-gen 88.07 is third-highest T-gen score (Table 8), behind GPT-4.1-mini (92.57) and GPT-4o (91.52), not the highest.

models.name: GPT-4.1-mini
models.key_results: Average 75.64 (Table 6): T-gen 92.57 (highest T-gen score, Table 8), T-conv 75.63, V-gen 64.30, V-conv 70.04. Also serves as the VLM judge for VQA scoring (Section 3, Figure 5).

models.name: GPT-4o
models.key_results: Highest overall average of all 12 models: 76.02 (Table 6 / Section 4.2 'Overall Performance'): T-gen 91.52, T-conv 73.95, V-gen 65.39 (highest V-gen), V-conv 73.20.

models.name: GPT-4o-mini
models.key_results: Average 73.19 (Table 6): T-gen 79.86, T-conv 75.57, V-gen 60.77, V-conv 76.54 (highest V-conv).

models.name: o1-mini
models.key_results: Headline SOTA figure cited in Abstract/Intro: average 75.58 (Table 6): T-gen 88.12, T-conv 81.82 (highest T-conv), V-gen 61.98, V-conv 70.40. Abstract: "even state-of-the-art models like o1-mini achieve only 75.58 average score."

headline_numbers: Best overall model GPT-4o averages 76.02% across all 44 tasks (Table 6, Section 4.2 'Overall Performance').

Abstract/Intro headline: o1-mini (framed as SOTA commercial model) achieves only 75.58 average, with open-source alternatives lagging ~10 points behind (Abstract; Section 1; best open-source Qwen3-4B = 67.04, Table 6). NOTE: Section 1's own prose names the best open-source model as 'Llama-3-8B-Instruct,' but this is a second internal inconsistency in the source paper — Table 6 shows Qwen3-4B is actually the best open-source model (67.04) and Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct is in fact the second-worst open-source model (51.59, only above Phi-3-mini-128k-instruct's 40.79). Table 6/Section 4.2 ('The best-performing open-source model is Qwen3-4B, with a score of 67.04%') is the paper's own more-detailed and load-bearing claim and should be treated as authoritative over the Introduction's offhand example.
Worst model Phi-3-mini-128k-instruct averages only 40.79% (Table 6, Section 4.2).

Under temperature=1: commercial models average 75.78% on StructEval-T generation vs. open-source models averaging only 8.58% specifically on TOML generation within that same task set (Section 4.2 'Generation vs. Conversion').

Several tasks are saturated (>0.95 for essentially all models): Text→Markdown, Text→HTML, and generation tasks for JSON/CSV/YAML also exceed 90% for most models (Abstract; Section 4.2 'Subtasks Analysis'; Table 8 shows JSON/CSV/YAML generation frequently >90-100 for strong models).

Several tasks are near-universally hard, all models scoring below 0.5: Text→Mermaid, Matplotlib→TikZ (Abstract; Section 1), plus Text→TOML, Text→SVG, Text→Vega, and conversions YAML→XML, CSV→YAML, Markdown→Angular (Section 4.2 'Subtasks Analysis').
VQA judge validity check: of 397 sampled VQA pairs, 88.92% were judged 'fair,' and the VLM judge (GPT-4.1-mini) correctly answered 98.30% of fair questions (Table 3, Section 2.1).
failure_modes: Section 4.2 error analysis on the worst model (Phi-3-mini-128k-instruct) identifies two concrete recurring structural failure patterns: (1) in TOML→YAML conversion, the model emits a malformed closing delimiter '|<|END_CODE|>' instead of the correct '<|END_CODE|>', which the strict delimiter-parsing evaluation penalizes heavily; (2) in CSV→JSON conversion, the model fails to capture hierarchical parent-child relationships encoded in CSV headers, producing structurally incorrect JSON. More broadly, the paper reports format-specific collapse (TOML generation near-zero for several open-source models, e.g., Phi-3-mini 0.00, Table 8) and visual/structural-reasoning failures on complex renderable formats (Mermaid, TikZ, Vega) attributed to the need for 'advanced visual reasoning capabilities' that are 'more prevalent in multimodal commercial LLMs' (Section 4.2 'Generation vs. Conversion'). The Limitations section separately notes the benchmark itself only covers static/non-interactive rendering (no button interactions, transitions, animations) — a scope limitation, not a model failure mode.
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper only tests and reports single forward-pass, zero-shot generation (Section 4.1: 'All tasks are evaluated in a zero-shot setting using consistent prompts and parameters'); there is no interactive, multi-turn, or few-shot/many-shot condition anywhere in the paper, and no comparison of one-shot vs. iterative/repaired generation is offered. The only conclusion drawable is about single-pass capability: models can reliably produce common/simple structures in one pass (JSON, CSV, HTML, Markdown generation all >90% for capable models) but cannot reliably produce less-common or structurally/visually complex formats in one pass (TOML, Mermaid, TikZ, Vega all <50% even for the best models) — the paper draws no claims about whether iteration, self-repair, or demonstrations would close this gap, since no such condition was run.

relation_to_candidate.position: StructEval sits on the single-shot / zero-shot end of the family's protocol axis: one prompt in, one full structured-output string out, scored automatically and non-interactively (Table 4 prompt template; Section 4.1). Its output shape is a full structure/sequence (an entire JSON/YAML/TOML/HTML/etc. document), not a single fact or label, matching c11's output shape (one canonical string). Its verifier is a composite of three deterministic-ish components — syntax parse validity (0/1), keyword/dot-path presence matching (proportion), and for renderable formats a VLM-judge visual-question-answering score (Section 3, Eq. 1-2) — which is graded/partial-credit scoring, in contrast to c11's strict whole-string 0/1 exact match against a single reference canonicalizer.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both are single-shot, zero-feedback tasks that ask a model to emit one complete structured-format string on the first (and only) forward pass, and StructEval's StructEval-T slice (JSON, XML, YAML, Markdown, CSV, TOML generation and conversion, Section 2.1) is exactly the format family c11 draws its 'canonical JSON' and record-serialization instance types from. Both surface format-specific difficulty cliffs (StructEval: TOML near-zero for open-source models, Table 8; c11: each invented house rule is its own stratum with its own difficulty). Both are motivated by the gap between 'fluent' and 'format-conforming' generation (StructEval Section 1; c11's canonicalizer premise).
relation_to_candidate.differences: StructEval scores by partial-credit keyword/dot-path matching plus syntax validity (and VQA for visual formats) — a document can be substantially 'right' and score 70-90% even with errors (Eq. 1-2) — whereas c11 requires exact whole-string match against a public-standard-deviating house convention, so a single wrong character (wrong key order, wrong quote style, wrong number format) is a total failure with no partial credit. StructEval evaluates conformance to *real, standard* formats (valid JSON, valid YAML per their own public grammars); it never asks models to follow *invented* rules that deliberately contradict the public standard (e.g., non-standard key ordering, non-standard escaping) — c11's central mechanism (house rules that beat RFC 8785 JCS on purpose) has no counterpart in StructEval. StructEval's task diversity is format-breadth (18-21 formats × 44 task types) rather than rule-breadth within one format; c11's diversity is rule-count (5-6 invented rules) within one serialization family, each independently checkable in the diff.
relation_to_candidate.what_c11_would_add: c11 would add: (1) a zero-partial-credit, fully deterministic exact-match verifier where StructEval uses graded/VLM-judge scoring, closing the gap StructEval's own Limitations/Section 3 leaves open around 'holistic' partial credit masking exactly-which-rule failures; (2) contamination-proof, infinitely regeneratable instances (StructEval's 2035 examples are fixed and could be memorized, versus c11's per-call invented house rules and random inputs); (3) direct attribution of each wrong output token to a specific violated rule (StructEval's keyword/dot-path score tells you *that* a key is missing, not which formatting convention was violated); and (4) explicit engineering against the 'not one-shot guessable' property — StructEval's formats are all publicly documented and could in principle be produced from training-data familiarity alone, while c11's invented deviations from JCS cannot be looked up.

corpus_claim: StructEval (Yang et al., 2025): its non-renderable slice (JSON/YAML/CSV/TOML) supplies c11's format taxonomy and below-ceiling floor evidence. (Known internal inconsistency found at acquisition: abstract says 18 formats, introduction says 21 - check which the paper's tables support.)
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported, with a correction on the count discrepancy. The paper does supply a non-renderable/text-only format taxonomy (StructEval-T: JSON, XML, YAML, Markdown, CSV, TOML — Section 2.1) and below-ceiling floor evidence directly relevant to c11 (e.g., TOML generation near-zero for several open-source models, Table 8; open-source TOML average 8.58% under temp=1, Section 4.2). On the count discrepancy: the extraction confirms both numbers appear verbatim in the source (Abstract: '18 formats and 44 types of task'; Introduction/Section 1: '21 distinct formats and 44 task types'), but every other count-bearing passage in the paper supports 18, not 21 — Section 2.1 ('covering 44 unique structure generation tasks across 18 structured output formats'), the Annotation Pipeline subsection ('covering 18 target formats and 44 distinct task types'), and Related Work Section 5.2 ('StructEval addresses these gaps by spanning 18 code and non-code formats'). Table 7 (Appendix A.1), the only table that itemizes formats directly, lists task rows by format-pair (e.g., Text→JSON, Text→CSV, CSV→JSON, JSON→XML, etc.) rather than a labeled format count, so it does not independently resolve the count, but the converging body-text evidence (4 separate passages) supports 18 as the paper's intended/predominant figure, with '21' in the Introduction as an isolated, uncorrected internal inconsistency. This is the authors' own error (confirmed in the extraction's Discrepancies section), not a citation or extraction error on our part.
baseline_relevant: Instance/dataset scale: 2,035 total examples across 44 task types (Table 1); StructEval-T generation alone uses 50 examples per task across 5 formats (JSON/CSV/TOML/XML/YAML), each with an average of 7.9 keywords for generation and 17.5 for conversion (Table 1, Table 7).

Prompt format that matters: the exact zero-shot instruction wrapper (Table 4) requires output delimited by literal tokens '<|BEGIN_CODE|>' / '<|END_CODE|>' with no markdown code fences and no explanatory text — the paper shows this delimiter format is itself a failure surface (Phi-3-mini emits a malformed '|<|END_CODE|>' and loses score, Section 4.2), suggesting our baseline should treat exact output-delimiter framing as a first-class, separately-tested convention akin to c11's own house-formatting rules.

Metrics/scoring: weighted combination of Syntax Score (0/1 parse or render success), Keyword Matching Score (proportion of dot-path keywords matched), and (for renderable formats only) VQA Score; StructEval-T score = 0.2·syntax + 0.8·keyword; StructEval-V score = 0.2·syntax + 0.1·keyword + 0.7·VQA (Section 3, Eq. 1-2) — all partial-credit, unlike c11's planned strict exact-match.

Floors/ceilings observed: ceiling in this benchmark is well below 100% even for the best model/format combos in aggregate (GPT-4o average 76.02%, Table 6), but individual 'saturated' subtasks reach ~99-100% (e.g., GPT-4o T→CSV = 100.00, T→JSON = 99.36, Table 8) — evidence that per-rule/per-format ceilings can be near-perfect even when aggregate scores are not, supporting c11's stratified-rule design where each rule's ceiling should be separately certified. Floor: open-source TOML generation floor as low as 0.00 (Phi-3-mini, Table 8) to ~16 (Phi-4-mini-Instruct = 16.12, Table 8; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct itself is only 6.76), i.e., near-random/failure on an under-represented real format even without any invented-rule deviation.
Inference/decoding config used: greedy decoding (temperature 0.0), unlimited max tokens, no caching, no batch API (Table 5) — a useful default-parameter reference point for a baseline run.

Contamination/data-release status: dataset was synthesized via LLM-based draft generation plus a two-pass human expert review using LabelStudio (Section 2.2), then fixed at 2,035 examples; this is a static, fixed-release benchmark (not regenerated per-eval), which is the opposite of c11's design goal of an infinite, non-memorizable pool — relevant baseline lesson: fixed corpora like StructEval are contamination-exposed over time, reinforcing why c11's regenerate-per-call design is a deliberate improvement, not merely a stylistic choice.

Format-difficulty dial evidence directly reusable for c11's stratification design: TOML, Mermaid, TikZ, SVG, Vega are consistently identified as the hardest formats across both open- and closed-source models (Section 1, Section 4.2 'Subtasks Analysis'), while JSON, CSV, YAML, HTML, Markdown are consistently 'saturated' — a ready-made prior for which format families to treat as high- vs. low-floor strata when designing c11's per-rule difficulty stratification.
quotes: "Our benchmark encompasses 18 formats and 44 types of task, with novel metrics for format adherence and structural correctness. Results reveal significant performance gaps—even state-of-the-art models like o1-mini achieve only 75.58 average score, with open-source alternatives lagging approximately 10 points behind." (Abstract)
"Our benchmark encompasses 21 distinct formats and 44 task types organized into two complementary subsets: StructEval-T ... and StructEval-V." (Section 1, Introduction)

"Before the evaluation, we feed the LLM with the questions q in the datasets with the corresponding prompt template defined in Table 4. We require the LLM to output the desired structured outputs between "<|BEGIN_CODE|>" and "<|END_CODE|>" so we can correctly parse the structured outputs for evaluation." (Section 3)
"All tasks are evaluated in a zero-shot setting using consistent prompts and parameters." (Section 4.1, Evaluation Models)

"Overall, GPT-4o achieves the highest average score of 76.02% among all 12 models. The best-performing open-source model is Qwen3-4B, with a score of 67.04%, trailing GPT-4o by approximately 10 percentage points." (Section 4.2, Overall Performance)

"Under a temperature setting of 1, commercial models attain an average score of 75.78% on StructEval-T generation tasks. In contrast, open-source models average only 8.58% on the same tasks for the TOML format." (Section 4.2, Generation vs. Conversion)

"Error analysis reveals two key failure modes for phi-3-mini-128k-instruct. First, in the TOML-to-YAML conversion task, the model frequently produces malformed closing tags, outputting |<|END_CODE|> instead of the correct <|END_CODE|>... Second, in the CSV-to-JSON conversion task, the model fails to capture hierarchical relationships." (Section 4.2, Overall Performance)

"SoEval (liu2024soeval) offers a fast, rule-based check for JSON and XML, but its flat schemas fail to reveal errors in deeper hierarchies... StructEval addresses these gaps by spanning 18 code and non-code formats, unifying generation, completion, and conversion tasks, and scoring outputs with fully automated structural and vision-based metrics." (Section 5.2, Evaluation of LLMs)

corrections: models[0] (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct): dossier claimed it was 'Best open-source model on some subtasks (e.g., T→TOML 6.76 vs Meta-Llama-3-8B 7.94 vs Qwen3-4B 7.44; Table 8)' — this was backwards. Table 8's T→TOML column shows Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct at 6.76, which is LOWER than both models it was being favorably compared against (Meta-Llama-3-8B 7.94, Qwen3-4B 7.44), and also lower than Qwen2.5-7B (13.22) and Phi-4-mini (16.12). Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct is in fact second-worst on T→TOML among the six open-source models (ahead of only Phi-3-mini's 0.00). Corrected to state this accurately.

models[6] (Gemini-1.5-pro): dossier claimed 'T-gen 88.07 (best T-gen among all models per Table 8)' — false and directly contradicted the dossier's own GPT-4.1-mini entry two rows below, which correctly claims the highest-T-gen title for its 92.57. Per Table 8, GPT-4.1-mini (92.57) and GPT-4o (91.52) both score higher than Gemini-1.5-pro (88.07) on T-gen; Gemini-1.5-pro is third, not first. Corrected to remove the false superlative and note the actual rank.

baseline_relevant floors/ceilings bullet: dossier claimed the open-source TOML-generation floor ranged '0.00 (Phi-3-mini) to ~16 (Llama-3.1-8B)' — the value 16.12 in Table 8's T→TOML column belongs to Phi-4-mini-Instruct, not Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (whose actual T→TOML score is 6.76). Corrected the attribution.

headline_numbers: added a note flagging a second genuine internal inconsistency in the source paper (distinct from the already-flagged 18-vs-21-format count issue) — Section 1's Introduction names 'Llama-3-8B-Instruct' as the best open-source model, but Table 6 / Section 4.2 clearly show Qwen3-4B is the best open-source model (67.04) while Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct is actually second-worst overall (51.59). This is the paper's own error, not a prior extraction/dossier error, but was previously unflagged and could mislead downstream readers who only skim the headline_numbers field.

Verified and found correct (no changes needed): all 12 models' Table 6 average/T-gen/T-conv/V-gen/V-conv figures against the source table; the Δ(o1-mini−Qwen3-4B) row arithmetic; the 18-vs-21 format count discrepancy characterization and its supporting 4 body-text passages; the protocol_mode classification (paper is genuinely single-shot/zero-shot throughout with no interactive or multi-turn track — the Limitations section's 'non-interactive formats' language refers to the rendered-UI format scope, not the evaluation protocol, and the dossier already correctly distinguishes this); the observation_content description of the Table 4 prompt template and delimiter tokens (verified verbatim); all 8 verbatim quotes (located and matched byte-for-byte in source, including the TOML-average and phi-3-mini error-analysis quotes); Table 1/Table 3/Table 8 statistics cited in baseline_relevant and headline_numbers (2035 examples, 44 tasks, 88.92% fair VQA / 98.30% fair-question accuracy, GPT-4o T→CSV=100.00/T→JSON=99.36, open-source TOML-gen average=8.58 arithmetic-checks against Table 8's T→TOML column); the saturated-tasks and hardest-tasks lists against Section 4.2 'Subtasks Analysis' prose; and the generation-vs-conversion exception pattern (commercial vs open-source) against Section 4.2 'Generation vs. Conversion' prose.
